transcript: Hundreds of people have been evacuated from their homes in southwest Iceland over fears of a volcanic eruption. Almost a thousand earthquakes have hit the area since midnight. They have been weaker than over the past two days though, as Sean Dilley reports. This is what life has been like lately in Grindavik. For days, thousands of tremors have scarred the land around the Rakejanes Peninsula. More than 3,000 residents have been evacuated from the small fishing town of Grindavik, not because of the quakes, but because scientists have warned about an imminent volcanic eruption. We are a little bit desperate, a little bit paralysed and sad actually. And if you start to think about all the time and energy that you have put in building up your home, it's just, it's just sad. These broken roads and paths only spell out part of the danger seismologists say is waiting underground. The magma is now on a shallow, very shallow depth. So we're expecting an eruption within a couple of hours shortest, but at least within a couple of days. The Rakejanes Peninsula is one of the most seismically active regions on the islands, but it had been dormant to volcanic activity. Until 2021, eruptions have, though, increased in recent years. In 2010, volcanic ash clouds drifted into Europe, grounding thousands of flights. For now, though, planning is focused here in Grindavik, where scientists say an eruption could happen in hours or days. Sean Dilley, BBC News.
